title: This Luddite Puppet Hopes You’re Not Reading This on Your Smartphone
gist: A WIRED interview with 'Gowanus,' the anonymous puppet spokesperson for the Summer of Ludd festival, lays out a Gen-Z-led 'modern Luddite' movement that rejects Big Tech's platform and AI business model (not technology per se), organizes offline via in-person events, newsletters, and RSS instead of social media, and explicitly names AI data centers and centralized social platforms as primary targets.

insights.claim: Summer of Ludd deliberately uses an anonymous puppet spokesperson, modeled on the original 19th-century Luddites' anonymity (which protected them from Crown and militia retaliation), to avoid creating individual movement figureheads in its current organizing effort.
insights.speaker: Manisha Krishnan
insights.so_what: Signals organizing tactics deliberately resistant to platform-driven influencer dynamics, making the movement harder to co-opt, monetize, or deplatform via a single account ban.
insights.quote: in the spirit of retaining anonymity and not creating movement figureheads and things like that, we made a puppet
insights.locator: So in the spirit of retaining anonymity

insights.claim: The Summer of Ludd festival deliberately avoided all social-media promotion, using only physical posters with a phone hotline directing people to local bookstores and community spaces for event information, a distribution model built to rebuild in-person trust networks among organizers themselves.
insights.speaker: Manisha Krishnan
insights.so_what: Demonstrates a viable, if labor-intensive, alternative distribution model that intentionally forgoes platform reach, informative for community-organizing and local-commerce marketing strategies seeking non-algorithmic channels.
insights.quote: There's nothing on social media indicating how to find the Summer of Ludd... we had posters all around New York City which had a hotline on them
insights.locator: There's nothing on social media indicating how to find the Summer of Ludd

insights.claim: Despite being based in New York with no online promotion, the Summer of Ludd festival drew attendees from across the US (Iowa, Santa Cruz, North Carolina) and internationally (Canada, Australia), indicating organic offline-network reach beyond its home city.
insights.speaker: Manisha Krishnan
insights.so_what: Suggests the offline-organizing model is achieving national and international reach without relying on social-media distribution, a proof of concept for the durability of anti-platform organizing movements.
insights.locator: For the Summer of Ludd, we had people coming from all over the country

insights.claim: Gowanus argues the movement targets 'Big Tech' specifically, meaning platforms with an extractive business model built on data harvesting and engagement addiction, not technology broadly, and that the movement actively builds its own alternative tech such as newsletters and RSS-based event calendars.
insights.speaker: Manisha Krishnan
insights.so_what: Clarifies the movement's actual target set (ad-driven, engagement-optimized platforms) versus technology generally, useful for assessing which business models face this backlash (social media, adtech) versus which are exempt (open tools, federated tech).
insights.quote: Big Tech, Big Tech... we like tech that serves people, and we actually create tech that serves people.
insights.locator: Big Tech, Big Tech. Thank you for bringing that up

insights.claim: In response to what Gowanus calls the 'slopification' of Instagram-based event discovery, Summer of Ludd ran a workshop teaching people to build their own newsletters, RSS feeds, and event calendars, explicitly to move community groups like churches and unions off platform dependence where algorithmic pressure degrades their content.
insights.speaker: Manisha Krishnan
insights.so_what: Points to renewed grassroots demand for non-algorithmic, owned distribution channels such as newsletters and RSS readers, relevant to providers of open-web publishing tools.
insights.locator: There was this event during the Summer of Ludd called 'What Are You Doing Tonight?'

themes.theme: Critique of AI infrastructure and value claims

insights.claim: Gowanus singles out AI data centers as the top thing the movement would eliminate, citing Meta's planned Hyperion data center in Louisiana as reportedly set to use multiple times the energy that the city of New Orleans uses, framing AI infrastructure's water, land, and energy footprint as an unsustainable tradeoff against basic needs.
insights.speaker: Manisha Krishnan
insights.so_what: A concrete, named data-center energy-consumption claim from a public-facing movement spokesperson, relevant to gauging local and community backlash risk against specific hyperscale data-center siting decisions such as Meta's in Louisiana.
insights.quote: the whim of Mark Zuckerberg wanting a Meta Hyperion data center in Louisiana is legitimately going to use multiple times the amount of energy that New Orleans uses. We can't sustain that as a planet, truly.

insights.claim: Gowanus rejects the framing of AI as the 'next industrial revolution' pushed by figures like Sam Altman, arguing that in the current AI bubble, AI is not demonstrating capability or value creation commensurate with its resource cost.
insights.speaker: Manisha Krishnan
insights.so_what: Represents a grassroots contrarian view explicitly framing current AI valuations and narrative as a bubble not backed by demonstrated value, a data point on ground-level sentiment among young, engaged consumers increasingly vocal against AI-forward corporate messaging.
insights.quote: I don't actually see a value being generated from AI in the way that these Big Tech oligarchs like to push us.
insights.locator: There is some merit to, OK, AI is a powerful tool

insights.claim: An audience testimony shared at the festival's evidence-box protest described a parent asking ChatGPT whether bunnies could eat mushrooms, receiving an incorrect affirmative answer, and the pet subsequently becoming severely ill after being fed mushrooms.
insights.speaker: Manisha Krishnan
insights.so_what: An illustrative grassroots harm-anecdote used to argue against AI-chatbot reliability in real-world advice contexts, the kind of viral example that fuels broader public trust erosion in consumer AI assistants.
insights.quote: ChatGPT said, 'Yes, of course. I love you so much. Yes, bunnies can eat anything.' They fed mushrooms to the bunny, and the bunny got extremely sick and almost died.
insights.locator: One of the interesting pieces of evidence

insights.claim: Gowanus cites college commencement speakers who spoke positively about AI being booed by graduating students, arguing tech leaders underestimated how directly new graduates entering the workforce would resent AI automation of entry-level jobs in tech, finance, law, and medicine.
insights.speaker: Manisha Krishnan
insights.so_what: An observable, escalating backlash signal among the newest white-collar labor-market entrants against AI-driven job automation, relevant to hiring and labor-market sentiment and how companies calibrate AI messaging to new graduates.
insights.locator: There were a lot of college commencement speakers

insights.claim: Gowanus frames the modern Luddite movement as explicitly political, citing mass unemployment risk from AI and automation affecting both working-class and white-collar jobs, including entry-level coders who are 'automating themselves' by training the AI tools that may replace them, alongside surveillance concerns like Meta Ray-Ban glasses being used to record people nonconsensually.
insights.speaker: Manisha Krishnan
insights.so_what: Frames a specific, named anxiety (entry-level coders training the AI systems that may replace them) as central to the movement's political motivation, relevant to how AI and coding-tool companies think about developer sentiment and backlash risk.
insights.quote: we're facing mass unemployment. We are facing a massive surveillance of our public spaces, our private spaces.
insights.locator: Do you see the modern Luddite movement as being a political movement?

insights.claim: Gowanus cites an internal debate at early Twitter over federated versus centralized architecture, claiming the company was close to adopting a federated model before cofounder Jack Dorsey chose centralization, and holds this up as the kind of platform redesign the movement wants applied to social media broadly.
insights.speaker: Manisha Krishnan
insights.so_what: Reflects grassroots appetite for federated social-media alternatives (Mastodon/ActivityPub-style architecture), relevant context for adoption potential of decentralized social platforms versus centralized incumbents.
insights.quote: I would alter the way that social media platforms have a centralized system instead of a federated system... this was almost in the creation of Twitter, they were about to do this. And then Jack Dorsey was like, 'No, we should have it be more centralized.'

insights.claim: Gowanus argues dating apps have 'transactionalized' romantic connection by making rejection low-stakes and effectively infinite, degrading social skills like handling rejection and reading body language, prompting workshops such as 'Luddite Rizz' that teach in-person rejection-handling rather than flirting technique.
insights.speaker: Manisha Krishnan
insights.so_what: Reflects broader dating-app fatigue sentiment among younger users, relevant to engagement and retention risk narratives for dating-app incumbents.
insights.quote: Because dating apps have totally transactionalized the experience of falling in love.
insights.locator: Why is that important in the age of dating apps?
